text:
# Manufacturing industry can still grow despite uncertainty

[Interview](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/interviews/index.html)

[Industry](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/industrie/index.html)

21/10/2025 • 5 minutes reading

[David Kemps](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/david-kemps/index.html)

The manufacturing industry is under national and international pressure. Raw materials are expensive, deliveries are uncertain and technical personnel are scarce. Yet ABN AMRO sector expert David Kemps sees opportunities. “Those who think ahead and organize their chains smartly can even grow now.”

“The industry is all about stability,” says David Kemps, Manufacturing sector expert at ABN AMRO. 'Companies must run their machines at a minimum of 80% occupancy to be profitable. As soon as deliveries falter or orders are lost, margins evaporate. And it is precisely that stability that is now under pressure due to geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainties.'

## Resilience as key

This instability affects entrepreneurs directly: parts arrive late or not at all, suppliers suddenly implement price increases or deliver lower quality. Kemps remembers a machine builder who had millions in stock during the corona period, but was unable to deliver due to the lack of a few circuits costing a few euros. “Then a detail undermines the entire security of supply and continuity.” According to Kemps, such an example shows how vulnerable the sector is to so-called single points of failure. 'A small component can become the bottleneck for an entire production line. That is why entrepreneurs must look much more closely at their dependencies: not only on suppliers, but also on transport, energy and personnel. To maintain control, he distinguishes four quadrants: purchasing and sales, short term and long term. 'Those who think ahead in all four areas increase their resilience. And that is crucial now.'

> “The trade war that Donald Trump is instigating is inhibiting investments in, for example, food and agri machinery and semicon.”

David Kemps

Sector Banker Industry

## Growth weakened

The fact that the sector is under tension is also evident from the forecasts. While ABN AMRO previously forecast 4% growth in 2025, this has been scaled back to 1%. A recovery to 3% seems possible before 2026, partly thanks to defense investments and increasing construction production. But the prospects remain fragile. “The trade war that Donald Trump is instigating is inhibiting investments in food and agricultural machinery, for example,” says Kemps. 'The semiconductor industry is also in danger of being disrupted.' The NEVI Purchasing Managers Index now shows export orders at a standstill and according to CBS figures, business confidence is at a low point. SMEs are extra vulnerable. “They are often completely absorbed by the operation and lack the capacity for scenarios and risk spreading,” says Kemps.

## Bronkhorst High-Tech: growing through local4local

Bronkhorst High-Tech from Ruurlo is the world leader in low-flow technology and produces approximately 100,000 measuring and control devices annually, good for a market share of 8%. With 900 employees, the company serves a wide range of sectors, from semiconductors to biotechnology. According to board member Fergus van Beek, the Dutch branch is reaching its limits. 'We can build another building here, but can we find two hundred extra employees? Grid congestion also acts as a brake.' In 2023, Bronkhorst decided to spread production capacity internationally, with assembly facilities in Singapore and the US. The German market worries Van Beek: 'The car industry is stagnating and investing less in innovation. Relying on one market or location is too big a risk today. Local4local requires a lot of organization, but makes us less vulnerable.'

## Combining short and long

The global unrest mainly affects the purchasing side: erratic steel prices, scarce chips and fluctuating transport costs. Entrepreneurs responded with short-term solutions such as extra inventory and multiple suppliers. “That provides relief, but is rarely structural,” says Kemps. Long-term strategies lie in nearshoring, cooperation with more stable countries or alternative raw materials. 'Remanufacturing and recycling are pure risk spreading. Companies that invest in this now create buffers against future shocks.'

text:
The global unrest mainly affects the purchasing side: erratic steel prices, scarce chips and fluctuating transport costs. Entrepreneurs responded with short-term solutions such as extra inventory and multiple suppliers. “That provides relief, but is rarely structural,” says Kemps. Long-term strategies lie in nearshoring, cooperation with more stable countries or alternative raw materials. 'Remanufacturing and recycling are pure risk spreading. Companies that invest in this now create buffers against future shocks.'

Collaboration in the chain is playing an increasingly important role in this. Companies are increasingly seeking partners to leverage joint purchasing power or to bring innovative materials to the market more quickly. “The old image of an isolated factory that arranges everything itself is making way for networks of companies that share risks,” says Kemps. The sales side is also changing. Export dependence is risky as countries protect their industries. In the short term, spreading customers and regions, and more competitive contracts, will help. In the longer term, local4local will become important: production closer to the sales market. 'This prevents levies and is in line with the sentiment of locally produced goods.' New revenue models, such as maintenance, training or Product-as-a-Service, also ensure more stable income.

> “Local4local prevents import duties and increases our chances.”

Fergus van Beek

Executive Board, Marketing & New Business at Bronkhorst High-Tech

## Making a difference

Yet there are companies that grow in this turbulence. They distinguish themselves with tight organization, up-to-date data and flexibility. “Anyone who links ERP to stock, planning and invoicing can manage quickly and based on facts,” says Kemps. In addition, solid agreements with suppliers and purchase contracts with customers make the difference. 'And companies that spread their risks and have a healthy financial buffer are stronger.' Agility is the common thread. Digitalization and automation increase predictability: AI checks, digital twins and data analysis make production more efficient. Sustainability fits in seamlessly with this. “Circular production and using alternative materials is no longer a luxury, but a prerequisite,” says Kemps.

> “Entrepreneurs who only look at today risk failing tomorrow.”

David Kemps

Sector Banker Industry

## Learning from multinationals

Major players have already learned to spread their chains. Parallel production lines, strategic inventories and contracts with room for price adjustments are commonplace. “What you see with the greats is that they have action options ready in every quadrant,” says Kemps. 'Entrepreneurs who only look at today risk failure tomorrow.' Medium-sized companies would also do well to draw up an annual strategy plan. 'Not a thick book, but a clear guideline. Those who do not choose a direction are led by the issues of the day.'

## Forward to 2030

Digitalization, sustainability and geopolitical fragmentation determine the course for the coming years. Production is moving closer to home, raw material flows are being monitored more critically and services are gaining ground. But staff shortages and grid congestion remain limiting factors. “Automation helps, but without young talent everything comes to a standstill,” Kemps warns. Still, he is optimistic. 'Dutch manufacturing companies are often leading and innovative. Provided they organize their chains smartly and make preparations in each quadrant, they can play a strong role in the coming years.'

Main image made by Jeroen Dietz

## These are your growth opportunities in 2026

The manufacturing industry faces challenges, but right now there are opportunities. Digitalization, AI and sustainability can make your company future-proof. In this video, business banker Melissa Geers explains where your growth opportunities lie in 2026.

## Read more in the industrial sector

The industry is a heavyweight in the Dutch economy. Export is an important driver for this, but at the same time creates uncertainty. There are also themes that have a positive influence on growth, such as cooperation in the chain, innovation and sustainability.

[View all articles](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/industrie/index.html)
